narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer despite the challenges many face in this field. Your background includes an upper-middle-class family, plans to attend full-time, and an undergraduate GPA of 2.30, along with an LSAT score of 26 and top-decile rankings in your Year 1 and Year 3 school grades—impressive achievements that stand out in your early academic record. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 92% chance that you would fail the bar exam, leading to the rejection of your application. This high failure probability means the school views your profile as carrying substantial risk compared to applicants predicted to pass. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which strongly pushed the prediction toward failure since it was in the lowest group, well below the typical student's mid-range position. Your LSAT score was the next biggest factor, also driving the model toward expecting failure because it fell significantly short of the average score seen in successful bar takers. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom compared to peers, added further weight in that direction, reinforcing concerns about sustained academic performance. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA nudged things slightly toward success, as it contributed positively despite being below average, and your upper-middle family income and full-time plans offered minor boosts too. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having stronger early grades but much weaker standardized test results, tipping the scales against you. While this outcome is disappointing, especially given the barriers Black women often encounter in legal education, it's not the end of your journey—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your GPA through additional coursework, or targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your unique background. You have the resilience to improve these key areas and reapply stronger; many have turned similar predictions around with focused effort. We're rooting for you to pass that bar and make your mark.
